metadata.career_relevance: String manipulation is in 40% of all DSA interviews. The #1 hidden performance killer: `result += char` in a loop → O(n²) due to String immutability. After this topic you'll never write O(n²) string code again, and you'll explain the complexity correctly in every interview.

hook.what_you_will_build: Deep understanding of Java String internals, complexity of every string operation, and StringBuilder patterns.

concept_explanation.operations_complexity:
**Java String operation complexity:**

```
s.length()        → O(1)  — stored field
s.charAt(i)       → O(1)  — array index
s.substring(l,r)  → O(r-l) — copies chars since Java 7u6
s.contains(t)     → O(n*m) — naive; KMP is O(n+m)
s.equals(t)       → O(n)   — char by char comparison
s.indexOf(c)      → O(n)   — linear scan
s.toCharArray()   → O(n)   — copy
s.split(regex)    → O(n)   — linear scan + compile

StringBuilder:
sb.append(c)      → O(1) amortized
sb.toString()     → O(n) — creates String
sb.reverse()      → O(n)
sb.charAt(i)      → O(1)
sb.length()       → O(1)
```

visual_aid.description: String pool diagram: String literals point to same pool object ('hello' shared). String s = new String('hello') creates separate heap object (avoid this). Comparison: s1 == s2 checks reference; s1.equals(s2) checks content. StringBuilder internal: char[] buffer that doubles when full (same as ArrayList amortized O(1) append).
visual_aid.alt_text: String pool, heap allocation, and StringBuilder buffer

worked_example.scenario: Build a string from character array — compare 3 approaches.
worked_example.problem_statement: Given char[] chars, build a String containing only alphanumeric characters.

step_by_step_solution.details: result += c creates a new String on each iteration. Step k copies k characters. Total: O(n²). At n=100K: 10^10 ops.
sb.append(c) is amortized O(1). sb.toString() is O(n). Total: O(n). At n=100K: 100K ops.
Pre-allocate char[], fill with valid chars, new String(chars, 0, len). O(n) time O(n) space.

things_to_notice: String is immutable — every += creates a new String object on the heap.
The Java compiler may optimize simple concatenations (s = 'a' + 'b') at compile time, but NOT concatenation inside loops.
StringBuilder.reverse() is O(n) — use it instead of building reversed string manually.
String.substring() is O(r-l) since Java 7u6 — it copies chars. In tight loops, prefer index arithmetic over repeated substring calls.

industry_examples.use_case: Normalize 10M product titles: remove special chars, lowercase, deduplicate spaces. StringBuilder pipeline: O(n) per title vs O(n²) naive. At n=200 chars × 10M titles: 2×10⁹ vs 4×10¹⁴ operations.

applications.interview_relevance: Every string interview: mention StringBuilder for building strings in loops. Mention substring is O(k). Mention equals vs == immediately. Interviewers test that you know String internals.
applications.real_world_workflow: String processing pipeline: (1) toCharArray() once for index access, (2) StringBuilder for output accumulation, (3) int[26] or int[128] for frequency counts (not HashMap), (4) toString() once at end.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using == to compare Strings. s1 == s2 compares references (usually false unless same pool object). s1.equals(s2) compares content.
common_gaps.remediation: Rule: ALWAYS use .equals() for String content comparison. s1 == s2 is almost never what you want. The only valid use: checking null (s == null).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student writes: if (s1 == s2) — will fail on most non-literal strings

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Calling s.substring(0, i) inside a loop thinking it's O(1). It's O(i) — copies i characters each time.
common_gaps.remediation: Track indices instead of creating substrings in loops. Pass (start, end) parameters or use character-by-character access via charAt().
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student uses s.substring() inside a loop → hidden O(n²)

key_takeaways: String immutability: every += creates new object → O(n²) in loops
StringBuilder: amortized O(1) append → O(n) for n appends
equals() for content, == only for null check
charAt(i): O(1). substring(l,r): O(r-l) — avoid in loops
int[26] for lowercase alpha frequency — O(1) per op, O(1) space
toCharArray(): O(n) one-time cost — pay it once at start
String comparison: s.compareTo() for lexicographic ordering

self_check_questions.question: Why is `result += char` inside a loop O(n²) instead of O(n)?
self_check_questions.answer: Strings are immutable in Java. The += operator doesn't modify the existing string — it creates a brand new String object that is a copy of the old string plus the new character. At step k, the new string has k characters, so the copy takes O(k) time. Over n steps: O(1)+O(2)+...+O(n) = O(n²). StringBuilder instead maintains a mutable internal char[] buffer. Appending is amortized O(1) (same as ArrayList) because the buffer doubles when full. Total for n appends: O(n). The final toString() is O(n) to create the immutable String — paid once at the end.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Pattern Matching — given text and pattern, find all occurrences. Naive O(nm) vs KMP O(n+m).
